try ``virtualenv --system-site-packages venv``

> Why would you recommend pygobject over pgi in this case?

Because pgi is currently so far away from feature complete that you can't run any reasonable sized/useful application with it.

>What I'm thinking, then, is that `InlineTableDict` will end up being more of an abstract base class (not really, since it'll be fine for 3rd parties to use it directly, but we won't), and we'll use a dynamically created subclass of both `_dict` and `InlineTableDict` for inline tables.

Just to note, I already made the changes relating to dynamic subclassing that you have suggested (in a separate branch).

> `InlineTableDict` needs to be a public interface, or else the power of `InlineTableDict` is only available to this library internally, which I think is less than ideal.

My comments below reflect my understanding of what you mean by "the power of `InlineTableDict`" but any clarifications are most welcome.

I'm not really sure that `InlineTableDict` is (or needs to be) a public interface. I view it as an implementation detail of the `preserve` flag. We could just as easily dynamically subclass `_dict` to add an `is_inline` method which returns `True`/`False` accordingly. The details don't particularly matter so long as `toml.dumps(toml.load(file_obj), True)` faithfully prints out inline tables as they existed in the original TOML file.

I do think that downstream users of the library may wish to know whether or not a given dictionary is an inline table, but that exposing that in the form of internal implementation details (in this case, a sentinel class) is less than ideal. I'm not sure what the best way to expose it is. I would prefer to take a more functional approach but I'm also concerned about extensibility beyond simply exposing information about inline tables. #77 comes to mind as something which would be better handled on an as-needed basis by a user of the library.

The API exposed by the library right now is rather simple and functional (in the sense of not particularly object oriented): `load`, `loads`, `dump` and `dumps`. Just like the `TomlDecodeError` and `TomlTz` classes,  the `InlineTableDict` class is an interface to the library only insofar as the library may expose these classes to its users as part of a returned value (or in raising an Exception). As a `toml` user, I would not rely too much on anything that exists in those classes but not their respective parent classes.

To take a cue from Python's `json` library, I think that creating `TOMLEncoder` and `TOMLDecoder` classes is one option to provide the flexibility that some users may need from this library. However, I think that such a change should be very well thought out. This is most likely outside of the scope of this particular PR.

Since there wasn't much feedback on my suggestions, I took the liberty of implementing a solution. The LineBuffer class now has a 'decoder' attribute, which takes a line and decodes it. One can replace LineBuffer.decoder at the class level, or at the instance level in each ServerConnection as appropriate. The default behavior is still to decode input using UTF-8 (with error="replace"), but clients may customize this behavior to their heart's content.

@eth0izzle thanks for this!

one of the rules I follow when developing a package is to make the most common usage simple, and for `pick`, that is "select one item from a list", and the new API `option, index = pick(options, title, min_select=1).one()` is just too complicated for me
maybe we could use `min_select` to distinguish if it's a multi select? eg:
`option, index = pick(options, title)` for one item, and 
`options = pick(options, title, min_select=2)` for multi select (`min_select>=2`)

To be honest, I wasn't aware that the empty line with the `-` meant a different account.

Modifying the parser to yield multiple transaction collections isn't all that trivial but it shouldn't be too hard to wrap it so the lines are parsed separately. I currently don't have the time to implement it so I'll leave it open until I have time to dig into it :)

Thanks for the support. The problem is how to deal with user defined numerical classes. Imagine that you do `X+Y` where `X` is a Pint quantity and `Y` is something else. 

(A) If `Y is a number, then
- if `X` is dimensionless, you want to return `X.to('') + Y`
- if `X` is not dimensionless, you want to raise a `DimensionalityError`.

(B) If `Y` is not a number, then you would like to return `NotImplemented`

Because we do not have good way to say if something is a number or not we decided to implement only A. We are open to suggestions in this regard.

But tell us more about your use case, maybe we can find a way to support it.

I took a quick look at this, it appears your issue asterisk.manager.ManagerSocketException: (0, 'Connection Terminated') can be avoided by catching the exception. 

The logoff is executed early in the script, but when the manager.close() is executed it saw that an instance was running and attempted to logoff that instance, but the connection got closed before it could complete. Thus the asterisk.manager.ManagerSocketException: (0, 'Connection Terminated') was raised.
You can add a try: except: block in the finally: section to overcome this error, but at this time I do not see any issue with the manager event itself relating to this. 

Remember that the asterisk manager interface is not the most robust when making multiple connections.
In testing it is rather easy to get connection errors simply by sending several requests back to back.
this should probably be achievable via logging.basicConfig(level=logging.debug) or such..

This behavior is active by default in version 2 but it need the schema to know the attribute syntaxes. In version 1 it was disabled by default.

There isn't any speed difference between the previous version and the new version. The code is basically identical.  It's on you to load mini-batches fast enough.  If you need to use multiple threads to do that then do so and pass them to the trainer via train_one_step().  

You also might just need to wait a minute.  Some datasets generate a high number of false alarms which cause the MMOD loss to be computed slowly, which happens on the CPU.  After a little bit that will go away and the GPU will be fully loaded.  Maybe that's all that's happening.

Right, you probably want to think about the training termination conditions.  Right now it's setup to do one pass over the training data, but that's often going to be not enough.  It's worth waiting to merge this PR until you have run a real example though the code and found out if it's really convenient and practical.  Overall I think the code looks good from a software engineering point of view.  But getting the numeric solver aspect of this kind of thing right can be tricky.  In particular, the number of iterations, learning rate schedule, and termination conditions are often tough.

If you want to see a more complex example of SGD with an automatic learning rate schedule you can look at the dnn_trainer object which uses the running_gradient (http://dlib.net/algorithms.html#running_gradient) object to estimate how many iterations have transpired without substantive progress.  It's a very robust statistical test and lets the dnn_trainer know when to reduce the learning rate.  I use it for all the DNN training I do and it works pretty flawlessly.  That doesn't mean it's the right thing here, but if you are looking for ideas it's a good start.

Thanks for reaching out. I'm able to decode the token without issue:

```python
import jwt
secret = 'qwertyuiopasdfghjklzxcvbnm123456'
token = 'eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..bkQEFDXsh8Ulwtv7rsbiBe3dmHVSHc5f-zWlu9KOldA'

jwt.decode(token, secret, audience='www.example.com')
``` 

You may be running into trouble with the `audience=` claim. PyJWT validates the `aud` claim in the token against the `audience=` arg to `jwt.decode()`. If you do not specify an `audience=` argument and an `aud` claim is found, you will receive a `jwt.exceptions.InvalidAudienceError` when running `decode()`

Alternatively, you could bypass `aud` verification, by passing `options={'verify_aud': False}` to `jwt.decode()` but that's not recommended since it bypasses a security feature. 

The preferred usage in production is to define a distinct `aud` claim value for your application and then ensure that all tokens meant for your application use this value and you pass the same value to `jwt.decode()`

```python
result = gdspy.slice(polylist, (sl), 0, precision=1e-6)
```
to see if that solves the problem.

In Python 3, `d.items()` [is a view object](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dictionary-view-objects), so it needs to be ~~`key=lambda d: tuple(d.items())`~~ `key=lambda d: frozenset(d.items())`.
On the other hand, while dicts and lists don't support hashing, they have "deep" equality comparison - of course, then you'd have to compare each new item with each past item, which is inefficient.

The problem is that to me it doesn't make sense to add zero padding when `zero_padding` is set to `False`. Because of this, to be consistent it should return an empty vector if `zero_padding` is `False`, and a zero-padded one when `zero_padding` is set to `True`.

## Demo

With the following setup:
```Python
import numpy as np
from speechpy.processing import stack_frames

def test_stacking(zero_padding):
    """Breaks subsets of array of [1..11] into groups of 3"""
    for length in range(12):
        audio = np.array(list(range(1, length + 1)))
        frames = stack_frames(audio, 1, 3, 3, zero_padding=zero_padding)
        print(str(frames).replace('\n', ''))
```

**Your proposed changes:**

```Python
test_stacking(False)
# []               #
# [[ 1.  0.  0.]]  #
# [[ 1.  2.  0.]]  # This breaks the pattern
# [[ 1.  2.  3.]]
# [[ 1.  2.  3.]]
# [[ 1.  2.  3.]]
# [[ 1.  2.  3.] [ 4.  5.  6.]]
# [[ 1.  2.  3.] [ 4.  5.  6.]]

# [[ 1.  2.  3.] [ 4.  5.  6.] [ 7.  8.  9.]]
# [[ 1.  2.  3.] [ 4.  5.  6.] [ 7.  8.  9.]]
```

```Python
test_stacking(False)

# []
# []
# [[ 1.  2.  3.]]
```

This use of pragmas is non-standard (in C99), but pycparser already has some support. I think the root issue here is this grammar rule:

````
    def p_statement(self, p):
        """ statement   : labeled_statement
                        | expression_statement
                        | compound_statement
                        | selection_statement
                        | iteration_statement
                        | jump_statement
                        | pppragma_directive
        """
        p[0] = p[1]
````

Because, in general, loops may have a single statement inside (which could be `compound_statement`) and here they see two, without braces.

A potential fix would be to allow pragmas as special case in such statements.

This problem is "solved" in the sense that `save` and `load` will now do something.  Unfortunately, numpy has no way of saving the fact that these are our particular type of user-defined type — quaternions — so you have to tell it the type when you load the data back in.  The array you get back after `load`ing will have dtype `void`, but you can change that by taking another view of the same data.  For example, the following code works correctly:
```python
import numpy as np
import quaternion

a = quaternion.as_quat_array(np.random.rand(5,3,4))
np.save("test.npy", a)
b = np.load("test.npy").view(dtype=np.quaternion)
np.array_equal(a, b)  # returns `True`
```
Note the `.view(dtype=np.quaternion)` where it's loaded.

```python
@concurrent(processes=36)
```

Retries should happen as with any other connection failure. Which means that for metadata requests, the retries will happen transparently. Consumer fetches should also retry transparently. For produce requests, the batch will use the `retries` configuration and may fail if the error is not resolved before the maximum retry attempts are exhausted.

Obviously you know the details of your requirements better than I do, but it seems like there are two approaches you could take. One would be to run `collectstatic` as part of the build phase and have the resulting files included with the rest of the build artifacts. The other would be to set a public acl and the appropriate cache headers on the individual files when they're uploaded to S3. It doesn't matter that the bucket as a whole isn't public: individual files within it can still be marked public so you don't need to use signed URLs.

It's very simple but gets its job done. Tested on Linux, FreeBSD and Windows successfully.

psutil already returns the command line splitted as a list, where the first item always contains the process name (with or without path). So, it's much easier working on the list rather then threating it as a string.

The only drawback is that the behaviour of psutil is not the same across the OSes. In fact, if no command line is available, it returns an empy list (correct) on Windows, while on Linux and FreeBSD (and OS X too?) returns [''].

While I was here, I fixed the not so common case where the command line contains line breaks (\n).

Anyway, please test. It would be nice if someone could test it on OS X, too.

The `Tree` class maintains a internal `_nodes` dictionary which is not updated on identifier and other changes. 

You may want to use a reimplementation of treelib called [anytree](http://anytree.readthedocs.io)

    from anytree import Node
    from anytree import find_by_attr


    root = Node('harry')
    Node('jane', parent=root)
    n = find_by_attr(root, 'jane')
    n.name = 'xyz'

    find_by_attr(root, 'xyz')  ### returns     Node('/harry/xyz')
    find_by_attr(root, 'jane')  ### returns None

HTH

May be obvious, but do note that if the URI you're passing to truffleHog's `git_url` argument starts with `file:///`, that is necessarily/by definition an absolute path URI, so you'd need to be providing the _full_ path to the file/dir you're scanning. For example:
```bash
$ echo $PWD
/Users/milo/code
$ ls
test-git-repo
$ truffleHog file://test-git-repo #THIS WILL FAIL
$ truffleHog file:///Users/milo/code/test-git-repo #THIS WORKS
$ truffleHog test-git-repo #ALSO WORKS
```

First, if this seems like a drawn out answer, please understand that I am an Ent, and I talk a long time to say things and I don't say anything unless I can take a long time to say it.  :smile: 

There are three pieces of knowledge to keep in mind about communicating between BACnet devices; the device's identity, the network that it lives on, and the MAC address of the device on its network.  Knowing the device identity there are services (Who-Is and I-Am) that allow you to find its BACnet address, the other two pieces.  You have given me two of the three pieces of this device, the device ID is 20 and the network is 200, but you don't know the MAC address of the device on its network.

BACnet is a little different than other other protocols, when you are communicating with another device and you are both on the same network, the address has no network number.  This is similar to not needing an area code for a phone number.  You only need the BACnet network number when there is a router between the network you are on and the network that the other device is on.  BACpypes calls these "local stations" and "remote stations".

So first find the complete BACnet address of the remote device by running the `DeviceDiscovery.py` sample application and at the prompt enter `whois 20 20` to find all the devices on your intranet with device identifier between 20 and 20.  You will get one response and it will look something like "20 is at 200:99 named snork".  The **99** is the MAC address of the device on its network, the **200** is its network, and **snork** is the object name of its device object, a.k.a., the device name.

The `Address` object takes all kinds of formats for addresses and tries to be smart about the kind of address you want.  `Address("1.2.3.4")` is an IPv4 address on the local network, `Address("200:1.2.3.4")` is an IPv4 address on an IP network other than your own, etc.  In this case I've assumed that the device you are talking to is on an MS/TP or ARCNET network that uses very small, single octet address, and **99** is its address.

    $ python
    Python 2.7.12 (default, Dec  4 2017, 14:50:18) 
    [GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
    Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
    >>> from bacpypes.pdu import Address
    >>> x = Address("200:99")
    >>> x
    <Address 200:99>
    >>> x.addrNet
    200
    >>> x.addrAddr
    'c'

Note that the address is bytes, not a string, it just looks like a string in Python2.  This is the address object that you use in your ReadPropertyRequest.

Your `LocalDeviceObject` contains the device identifier that you want to use for your application when other devices want to talk to you.  The BACnet address of your "device" is a special form called a BACnet/IP address and it is the IP address of the machine you are running the BACpypes application, along with the UDP port number (normally 0xBAC0, 47808).

_Note that you don't know the network number of the network you are on!_  The knowledge of the topology is built into the configuration of the routers, and your application is not a router.  So this device that you are talking to will know because the router will fill in your network number in the network layer header (the SNET/SLEN/SADR fields), just like you'll know its network when the I-Am response comes back in the device discovery process.

(There are new services to discover what network you are on, but they are not available in BACpypes yet.)

As a quick work-around, you can create a `SoundFile` object (ideally in a `with` statement), use `len()` to get the number of frames and then use the [`blocks()` *method*](http://pysoundfile.readthedocs.org/en/0.8.1/#soundfile.SoundFile.blocks) (instead of the `blocks()` function).

```python
import soundfile as sf

with sf.SoundFile('myfile.wav') as f:
    frames = len(f)
    for block in f.blocks(1024):
        # do something
```

> Of course I could implement my own overlap, but by the same logic, I could implement my own `blocks`.

No, that's faulty logic.
If that were logically sound, the function `blocks()` would also have to implement arbitrary windowing, arbitrary time-frequency transforms and arbitrary signal processing in general. Basically Turing-complete function arguments. That's clearly absurd.

So let's not extrapolate from one feature to another but just consider each feature separately.

Of course it's not strictly necessary to provide `blocks()`, but I still think it makes sense, because it avoids having to check in each iteration if the file is finished yet. And a generator of NumPy arrays is a really nice interface to external code.

The big difference with the overlap functionality is that this could be implemented generically (because of the nice "sequence of arrays" interface). It shouldn't be specific to the `soundfile` module.
It would be a really nice tool to have in a signal analysis module, along with tools for windowing and similar helpers.
And the good thing would be that this could also be used for other sequences of NumPy arrays, e.g. from live sound card input or an RTP network stream. Or in the trivial case just (a one-element sequence of) one big fat array.
If the "overlapper" were a separate tool, it would also be trivial to implement (external to the `soundfile` module) what you (@bastibe) mentioned [above](https://github.com/bastibe/PySoundFile/pull/209#issuecomment-334405078):

> It might be worthwhile for `blocks` to optionally read larger chunks and split them into blocks internally.

For `soundfile.blocks()` you should use a `blocksize` that makes sense for *reading from the file*, since that's the realm of the `soundfile` module. The block size may be much larger than the size you would like to use for spectral analysis.
The resulting generator could be fed into a generic "overlapper", which would turn it into (potentially overlapping) blocks of a different length.
Nice, clean separation of concerns. Nice modularity and composability.

BTW, in its current form, isn't there a feature missing for your use case?
Shouldn't it be possible to stop the iteration at the last *full* block, discarding the last *partial* block?
Otherwise the partial block doesn't get the correct windowing and the results are wrong because of an unwanted rectangular window?

Having said all that ... while thinking about all this for a while, I found yet another option:

**option 6, get rid of `out`, without replacement**

This would allow us to keep the overlap feature while still being "clean".
The reason for implementing `out` in the first place was symmetry with `read()`. But I think we could live without it. Whoever needs to write into pre-allocated memory can use the `read()` method directly.

Thank you for your reply. I finally succeeded with using the packages of cryptography and pyasn1. It might appear a bit awkward, but it works for me. This is what I did so far:

Importing the needed modules:
`from cryptography import x509
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
from pyasn1.codec.der.decoder import decode as der_decoder
from pyasn1.type.univ import BitString`

Reading the x509 certificate:
`cert = x509.load_pem_x509_certificate(open(my_cert, 'rb').read(), default_backend())`

Retrieving the public key from certificate:
`pubdata = cert.public_key().public_bytes(cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization.Encoding.DER,cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization.PublicFormat.SubjectPublicKeyInfo)`

To parse the ASN.1 information I used the following code from
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21954512/parse-ec-public-key-with-pyasn1
With that I'm able to get the binary code of the EC-Point in string representation:
`class curve(pyasn1.type.univ.Sequence):

            componentType = pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedTypes(
                pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedType('type',pyasn1.type.univ.ObjectIdentifier()),
                pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedType('name',pyasn1.type.univ.ObjectIdentifier())
            )
        
        class EcPublicKey(pyasn1.type.univ.Sequence):
            componentType = pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedTypes(
                pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedType('curve', curve()),
                pyasn1.type.namedtype.NamedType('publickey', pyasn1.type.univ.BitString())
            )
der_code,rest = der_decoder(pubdata, asn1Spec = EcPublicKey())`

Finally I had to prepend an identifier for the regarding uncompressed elliptic curve (0x0461):
`str_code = '10001100001%s' % (str(der_code['publickey']))`

As `str_code` contains now the correct EC point, I converted it to byte code and used it as _other_point_ parameter for python-pkcs11: `derive_key()`. 
Although this solution only fits to a certain curve, it works so far for me.

`notify_order` (like the other notification methods) is meant to receive notifications, not actually for executing operations. Although nothing prevents it. In a single `next` cycle, several notifications may be sent to the strategy and that's why I would do everything in `next`.

The code you quote

```python
if self.order:
    return  # if an order is active, no new orders are allowed, in this case two orders could be active
```

is a simple sentinel for simple samples. If `order` is not `None` it is because it has been returned by either `buy` or `sell` and not yet set to `None` when a cancelation or completion is received in `notify_order`

Hi,

Sorry -- I missed this. This is a meta-data element that is added when OrderedDict is used with the existing JSON encoding here. The reason for this is that there may be re-ordering of the entries in the JSON object - since it has no strict ordering.

At the moment, there is not a flag to turn this off -- one could be added. Additionally, I am looking at implementing a serialisation format compliant with the IETF JSON encoding for YANG.

Cheers,
r.

There's a comment that points to ECMA 262 just above, and indeed http://www.ecma-international.org/ecma-262/6.0/#sec-date-time-string-format seems to mandate a `Z` to make it explicit that the datetime value is in UTC.

> ECMAScript defines a string interchange format for date-times based upon a simplification of the ISO 8601 Extended Format. The format is as follows: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss.sssZ

The timezone information is preserved. It's just encoded with a more terse (and perhaps slightly obscure convention).

The way that we have been trying to avoid the issue of comparing incomparable objects was to make new ways of distinguishing and sorting them. The idea was to enforce stable sorting, where even if the ranges equal, one Interval will always sort lower than the other. 

The advantage of the above is that during the execution of the program, the relative ordering should not change when we edit the IntervalTree. If two intervals had equal ranges, and one sorted below the other, it should **not** flip and suddenly sort above the other, simply because the tree structure changed and needed to be resorted. Note the "should."

The disadvantage is that if the data field of an interval was modified, this could change the sorting order. This is serious, because it is quite likely that users will want to use dynamic objects in the data field, whose sorting order can therefore change without notice to the IntervalTree. (This is probably part of why Python 3 became more strict about ordering many objects. If so, this is all the more reason not to reinvent sorting.)

If we want to have truly stable sorting, the sorting therefore **cannot** be dependent on dynamic objects in the data field. The most elegant way to guarantee this is to not let sorting depend on the data field at all, and instead rely on the `id()` values of Intervals as tiebreakers where the Interval ranges equal.

This is a backwards-incompatible change, which if implemented, must be postponed to a new major version.

`data` is allowed to be a list. The issue here is that with `IntervalTree(intervals)`, the initializer expects an iterable of `Interval` objects. If you have a list of tuples, use this instead:

`tree = IntervalTree.from_tuples(intervals)`

Yes, attrs (and my proposed code) would add a `__hash__`. 

The way attrs and my code work is that they look through the current class and all base classes (in reverse MRO order) and find the fields. In attrs case, a field is something with a default value of `attr.ib()`, in my case it's something in `__attributes__` (that is, it's a member variable annotated with a type). The generated `__hash__` for the current class would include the hash of all of the fields in this and all subclasses. Non-fields are ignored in my case, and I assume for attrs, too.

So:

    >>> @dataclass(hash=True)
    ... class B:
    ...   i: int
    ...
    >>> @dataclass(hash=True)
    ... class C(B):
    ...   j: int
    ...
    >>> hash(B(10)) == hash((10,))
    True
    >>> hash(C(1,2)) == hash((1,2))
    True

The generated `__hash__` for `C` would be `return hash((self.i,self.j))`.

Without frozen=True, this is how my code currently works:

    >>> @dataclass(hash=True)
    ... class C:
    ...   i: int = 0
    ...
    >>> hash(C())
    -1660481949
    >>> hash(C()) == hash((0,))
    True
    >>> hash(C([]))
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "<string>", line 2, in __hash__
    TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

attrs has the following rules:

If `hash=None` (default), the `__hash__` method is generated according how `cmp` and `frozen` are set.
- If both are `True`, attrs will generate a `__hash__` for you.
- If `cmp` is `True` and `frozen` is `False`, `__hash__` will be set to `None`, marking it unhashable (which it is).
- If `cmp` is `False`, `__hash__` will be left untouched meaning the `__hash__` method of the superclass will be used (if superclass is object, this means it will fall back to id-based hashing.).

I think these are reasonable rules.

Hey @jjjake, thanks for reviewing. 

The python modules are inside the .snap, but these packages are more intended for final users than for developers. A developer would install your library from pip in a virtualenv and use the python interpreter. That's doable from the snap, but with multiple limitations because for security the package is not installed like traditional debs are. It is just mounted read-only in the `/snap/` dir. That makes it a little complex to write a script that combines your modules and other third party modules. It's doable, but definitely a virtualenv is better for that. The snap is for the command line tool.

I didn't reserve the name. As a safe guard to avoid people taking ownership of existing projects in the ubuntu archive, all the names of deb binaries are reserved. Apparently, [duckduckgo has a binary called ia](https://packages.debian.org/stretch/amd64/python-duckduckgo2/filelist). That's not a big deal, if you want ia you just have to fill the registration form explaining your claim to the name, and we'll assign it to you. Or we can rename the snap to internetarchive as you mentioned, and ia can be an alias ([work in progress](https://forum.snapcraft.io/t/improving-the-aliases-implementation/18)). This second alternative doesn't require any approvals from store admins.

One of the ideas of snaps is to avoid the overhead of distro maintainers. So it would be great if you own the snap in the store. You can automate the continuous delivery with a couple of clicks in https://build.snapcraft.io or you can use [travis](https://snapcraft.io/docs/build-snaps/ci-integration) or something else. I'd be happy to help you there if you need a hand.

And the project is in active development. We are very eager to hear from people using snaps, so if there's a feature you are missing or something that could be easier, please let us know and we'll put it in our backlog.

I'm pretty sure this is the same thing I saw in #335 - something is changing with the EFS service lately. In #335, EFS in us-west-2 (where it's not supported) used to fail with a ``EndpointConnectionError`` (that awslimitchecker handles) but is now failing with an ``AccessDeniedException``. EFS is [not supported](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/rande.html#elasticfilesystem-region) in sa-east-1 either, and it seems that what happens when we try to connect has changed (but, strangely, is different from the new behavior in us-west-2).

I have a ticket open with AWS for this through my employer's Enterprise account, since we're also affected by it, but I haven't heard anything back yet. I'll ping them back today about it...

We came up with a temporary workaround for our purposes, where we just don't check EFS in regions that don't support it (until AWS fixes this).

To modify the example code you gave above to do the same thing:

```python
c = AwsLimitChecker(
    account_id='111111111111',
    account_role='myrole',
    region='sa-east-1'
)

services = [s for s in sorted(c.get_service_names()) if s != 'EFS']
result = c.check_thresholds(service=services)
```

We just call ``AwsLimitChecker.get_service_names()`` and then pass all service names but EFS as a list to the ``service=`` parameter of ``check_thresholds()``.

Do you mind if I close this issue as a duplicate of #335 and change the description of that to be more general, about handling the new issues when connecting to EFS?

This library is just to identify faces. It doesn't attempt to check if the person is living or a picture. That's a separate problem called liveness detection.

One possible approach is to add code to your application to make the person blink and detect that they blinked: https://www.pyimagesearch.com/2017/04/24/eye-blink-detection-opencv-python-dlib/

But then that can still be fooled by a pre-recorded video.

Liveness detection actually a pretty hard problem that no one has solved with 100% accuracy. Apple uses a 3d model (instead of 2d) with FaceID to try to improve this, but even that isn't 100% perfect.

`n_neighbors` is the number of neighbors it looks for using the KNN algorithm to find the nearest neighbor. If you read up on the KNN algorithm itself, it will make more sense.

If you want to try a different approach for your IP camera, maybe check out this method instead: https://www.pyimagesearch.com/2018/06/18/face-recognition-with-opencv-python-and-deep-learning/

It adds a simple voting algorithm that might improve accuracy in your use case.

Hey @baraline, thanks for the details issued. There's a folk theorem of computational statistics that says "When you have computational problems, often there’s a problem with your model". Looking at your output of the `fit`, we can see that the model is on the precipice of fitting: the `delta` explodes and we have to taper the `step_size` down. When we break the dataset into K pieces, we probably fall over the precipice into unstable convergence areas due to the smaller sample size.

So along with the usual checks that lifelines does (which includes some variance calculations and correlations _with_ the duration column), I would also suggest:

1. Looking for high (>.95) correlations between covariates
2. linearly-dependent covariates (harder to find)

Are you able to share the dataset with me? As lifelines matures, these datasets that cause convergence problems become rarer and rarer, and I like to test with them locally to build new "protection" into lifelines.

> ..it should be up to consumer to fix his Python environment by
> installing win-unicode-console and enabling it (on Windows) in his
> sitecustomize. I.e. I don't think it is a good idea to import
> win-unicode-console in a consumer script.

Yes, in the ideal world `win-unicode-console` should be completely
transparent for the Python code that performs Unicode I/O and
`win-unicode-console` would be shipped with Python. It is too
intrusive otherwise to force users to modify their global environment
in a way that might break other Python scripts. `win-unicode-console`
should provide a solution even for those people who won't modify
`sitecustomize`, `usercustomize`, and the like. Usually, I recommend
`py -mrun` approach but it is nice to have an option to enable it from
within a script too.

> ..But you can still do it in a multiplatform way – just make it
> conditional, testing the platform.

Yes, I can do it as well as all other developers that use your module
and need a cross-platform support.  Or we can do it in a
single place:

    try:
        from win_unicode_console import streams, console, readline_hook
    except (AttributeError, ImportError):
        enable = lambda **kw: None
        disable = lambda: None
    else: # the rest of the win-console-unicode/__init__.py

that is all: `win-unicode-console` should provide useful behavior on
Unix, namely: it should do nothing there -- in particular, it should
avoid `ImportError`.

Any additional behavior would be an _enhancement_. `win-unicode-console`
mission: *"enable Unicode input and display when running Python from
Windows console"* could be _extended_: *"enable Unicode input and
display when running Python."*

Currently, Python unreasonably respects misconfigured ASCII locale
settings. Usually, `PYTHONIOENCODING` could be used to force utf-8.
As an enhancement, `win-unicode-console` may provide a similar (but
perhaps more aggresive and less general) option from within a
script. See [related discussion][1].

Example:

    #!/usr/bin/env python
    import codecs
    import sys
    import win_unicode_console # $ pip install win_unicode_console

    broken_environment = (sys.getfilesystemencoding() == 'ascii' and
        codecs.lookup(sys.stdout.encoding) == codecs.lookup('ascii'))
    win_unicode_console.enable(use_utf8=broken_environment)
    print(u"\N{SNOWMAN}")

The only difference compared to the current implementation is that it
enables utf-8 streams on top of binary API instead of utf-16le streams
on top of Unicode API. Use-case: enable Unicode input and display in
Python REPL started via ssh on a remote Linux box.

The preferred solution would be to fix the environment but we live in
an imperfect world where it is not always possible.

`use_utf8` is an enhancement. Just "do nothing on Unix" option would
be fine.

[1]: https://bugs.python.org/issue19846#msg205564

I'm able to reproduce this. Appears to have been introduced in https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/commit/12aba225843023cc7355406e7c984eec733f4a2d when templates were added to the application. Django version should not matter here.

**To reproduce this:** Override `registration/activation_email.txt` but do *not* override `registration/activation_email.html`. The default `.html` template will be used in the emails.

**Cause:** The issue is that the code first looks for the `.txt` version of the template, and then checks for the `.html` version. It finds your `.txt` version just fine, but then finds the newly included `.html` template, and uses that instead.

Before https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/commit/12aba225843023cc7355406e7c984eec733f4a2d, no templates were included, so if you never created a `.html` version, your `.txt` version would be used.

@grillazz with your example above, it should now work but you'll have to define the DRF serializer field for each of your model fields explicitly, e.g.:

```python
from rest_framework import serializers

class UserGuessModelSerializer(ModelSerializer):
    some_field = serializers.CharField()
    some_other_field = serializers.CharField()
    # etc etc, do them all

    class Meta:
        model = UserGuess
        fields = '__all__'
```

This is because DRF contains a direct mapping to django models to choose the serializer field (https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/bb56ca46ed6c07db0146dbdc61c672ff25f127de/rest_framework/serializers.py#L766)

My idea with pyproject.toml is that it is meant to be used not only for building the package (as per [PEP 518](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/)), but as already stated there, use the ``[tools.z3c.dependencychecker]`` sub-table to store the these meta dependencies.

See the [two paragraphs above this header](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/#rejected-ideas).

I expect that developers will start to use pyproject.toml more and more as python and its ecosystem pushes more bits in it.

And as it is a TOML file it should be easier to parse and use, so rather than setup.cfg I would prefer that one. There is some rationale on that PEP about why TOML as well.

#### Input

For the input source I like:
```
$ cat event.json | chalice invoke -n myfunction
```

I don't think the `--event-type` or the HTTP specific options are necessary. I'd rather just keep it dead simple and have it invoke the lambda function and pass your specified payload to the function as is. If you want to invoke lambda through API gateway use httpie. If you really want to see what is being sent from lambda to api gateway you can use `chalice invoke -n api_handler` and pass a payload like this:
```
{
   "method": "GET",
   "path": "/"
}
```

#### Output
Something else to consider is the output. We get back the actual response from lambda, one component of which is a payload streaming body. My thought is we just ignore all the metadata and response keys and write the `Payload` to stdout. The only exception is if we have a key/value pair: `"FunctionError": "Unhandled",`. In which case we get back a body that looks like this:

```
{"errorMessage": "<__main__.LambdaContext object at 0x7fc24750de10> is not JSON serializable", "errorType": "TypeError", "stackTrace": [["/var/lang/lib/python3.6/json/__init__.py", 238, "dumps", "**kw).encode(obj)"], ["/var/lang/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", 199, "encode", "chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)"], ["/var/lang/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", 257, "iterencode", "return _iterencode(o, 0)"], ["/var/runtime/awslambda/bootstrap.py", 110, "decimal_serializer", "raise TypeError(repr(o) + \" is not JSON serializable\")"]]}
```
I'd like to print this information like a standard python error/traceback to stder. I think that would make for the cleanest experience debugging a misbehaving lambda function.

I just finished implementing backprop through einsum for an educational tool I'm writing, and was able accommodate all einsum cases. I handle traces by writing to a strided view along the diagonals of a tensor of zeros, which is equivalent to contracting against kronecker-deltas.

This permits all manner of crazy traces, like `'ijji,kji,jj-> kj'`.

I'm not sure if I'll find time to write up a PR for autograd, but I can try piece one together. I have a couple of questions: 
- Is there any reason why you handle the `"ijk"` and `op0, sublist0 ...` separately? The latter can be normalized to the `"ijk"` convention using `numpy.core.einsumfunc._parse_einsum_input`. This would also lift your constraint of requiring a sublist_out parameter.
- Do you purposefully avoid using `numpy.broadcast_to` in the 'naked-sum' case (uncontracted-sum over axes)?  I ask, because my method for back-propping through a trace requires that the gradient be broadcast along axes that were reduced via uncontracted-summation. (Also, contracting explicitly against an array of ones is much slower ~100x-1000x).

@dllahr: The second to last commit is a big one, so it'd be good to get your thoughts.

We're proposing to rename `concat_gctoo.py` to `concat.py`, `slice_gct.py` to `subset.py`, and `slice_gctoo.py` to `subset_gctoo.py`.

The reason is to stay consistent with `parse.py`: if a script works on either GCT or GCTx, the file name shouldn't have GCT or GCTx in the name. We had to change slice to subset because slice is a built-in Python thing. If a script works on a GCToo object, then it has gctoo in the name (i.e. `subset_gctoo`). However, `concat.py` (formerly, `concat_gctoo.py`) works on GCT and GCTx files: thus the name change.

I think I've fixed references to these 3 scripts everywhere, but I'm sure I might have missed something. We've also added deprecation warnings to the old files so that it's clear what the new syntax is in.

Let us know what you think.

The first item in ct is -1, nd.pick select 0.9417475 rather than 0.9575423. In most detection model, 0 means background class. So I think nd.pick takes -1 as 0 and thus treats _ignore_ in _ct_ as _background_.

from

```
rank = (cls_loss * (pos - 1)).argsort(axis=1).argsort(axis=1)
```

```
rank = (cls_loss * ((ct !=0) - 1)).argsort(axis=1).argsort(axis=1)
```

Generally speaking when you run `pipenv lock` you’re resolving the entire dependency graph. For Django projects this can be quite large and complex— I use Django at work and it is quite a process. To be quite frank unless you’re developing inside a docker container it doesn’t make a lot of sense to run `pipenv lock` in your compose script. This is kind of defeating the purpose of pipenv. 

If you run `pipenv install` or `pipenv install —dev` locally for you dev process, you will already have a lockfile you can deploy. That’s the whole purpose here— you already have an isolated python environment in a virtualenv which is managed by pipenv, so it can handle dependency graphs across platforms, os and whatnot. Since I imagine you install things locally when you develop, there’s really no reason at all to force docker to re-resolve a dependency graph that already was resolved. 

Resolvers are hard, and sometimes in python we need to download the whole package to parse it’s `setup.py` to crawl the dependency graph. If you don’t pass a lockfile to your deployment you’re basically handicapping yourself.

It’s not about having multiple entries or not. If you run resolution on platforms A or B, you get one result, but if you run resolution on platform C, you get a different result. Since these platforms are different and resolve differently, and pipenv uses a resolver to _resolve_ dependencies (that is the nature of the tool after all), platforms A and B don’t even know that anything is different about platform C. You see, this knowledge would be impossible to have unless you _were_ platform C. That’s the nature of how pythons packaging ecosystem operates unfortunately. 

This would be no problem if in fact the difference were indicated in _markers_ but markers are not the same as version numbers or post releases. Pip doesn’t even touch the wheels for incompatible platforms, so we will literally never know about a version discrepancy like this. So I know you might think you’re asking for something simple (judging by your request that we simply add another entry), but you aren’t. Please appreciate that we don’t enjoy having issues like this, if it were easy to fix we would have done it already.

@ryan-blunden with the gist you linked, you should be getting an error, but it seems like you're getting the _wrong_ error.  If you are passing `--system` you can't install from a pipfile, you _must_ have a lockfile unless you pass `--skip-lock`.  If you pass `--deploy` you can't install from an out-of-date lockfile (one that is out of sync with your pipfile) which means you must both include your pipfile AND your lockfile and they must be in sync.

If you want to do the resolution step in a container you need to drop the `--deploy` flag and add the `--site-packages` flag.

The behaviour is that Qt looks for `qt.conf` in the same directory as the current executable. Have a look at what executable your IDE is actually launching, it might be different from `c:\Python27\python.exe`. I've heard of this happening with PyCharm, for example, when running in debug versus release mode.

You can print the current executable like this.

```python
import sys
print sys.executable
```

There should be a `qt.conf` in the same directory, and it should contain lines pointing to where your PyQt5 installation is at.

- http://doc.qt.io/qt-5/qt-conf.html

It can get really complicated, so I don't blame you for getting frustrated. I've been there. :( 

See the [Bundling](https://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5/wiki/Updating-the-repository) section of the wiki here for the full story.

- https://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5/wiki/Updating-the-repository

IMO, commands shouldn't behave differently based on what the output format is.

In the case of:
```
$ az ad sp create-sp-for-rbac --out [table|tsv]
Service principal has been configured.
  ClientID:           http://azure-cli-2016-10-18-14-14-45
  Password: a5987e3d-ac0a-4429-a3ee-26cdd05e518c
  TenantID:                72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47

...
```
Now, the command has made it impossible for me to do something like the following in my script:
```
$ az ad sp create-sp-for-rbac --query "client_secret" --out tsv
8b4b3ea3-ffa6-4061-8f4a-123456789012
```
It ignores my specified output format and tries to be user-friendly so I can't use query to get the field I'm interested in.

Also, in the case of:
```
$ az ad sp create-sp-for-rbac --out json
{
    "client_secret": "8b4b3ea3-ffa6-4061-8f4a-123456789012", 
    "client_id": "a1b1fd3b-94d3-4376-8e07-e8fbefa0f61e", 
    "app_id_uri": "http://yugangw112"
}
```

The command can still print out the user friendly messages for json format. It will get sent to STDERR so wouldn't negatively impact scripting scenarios.

Is use to serve the app with waitress (which is used in production only)

```
[uwsgi]
socket = 127.0.0.1:8888
```

We will never have to use such a setup because in production we will use file sockets.

```
socket = /run/wsgi/kinto.sock
```

```
kinto.http_host = localhost:8888
```

This one is the public address when kinto have to name itself.

So in production the setup will be:

```
[uwsgi]
socket = /run/wsgi/kinto.sock

kinto.http_host = kinto.services.mozilla.com
```

As you can see we need to keep all of this settings.

``kinto.http_host`` should never ever looks like ``0.0.0.0:8000`` because 0.0.0.0 is not a correct ip address to name a kinto server.

Please not: IMO it should be an explicit step to pollute the conda environment with non-conda (e.g. pip, r,...) installed packages.

Adding an automatic step to call pip is also very problematic in the case that there is an pip package with the same name as the conda package but with different content. In cases like unavailable conda packages (e.g. for certain python versions), this means that a wrong package is installed. If this even happens for requirements, this would be really bad.

Conda is also used to install r packages. While these packages are currently easily identifiable via a `r-` prefix, it could also mean that requesting `conda install r-unavailable` would install a python package with that name. Conda can potentially be used to install more than python packages and should not gain a default fallback to only one package type.

IMO a better idea is to add an explicitly hint to pip (or other package manager) and not only to `conda search`. Another step would be a community supplied central conda channel, so that more python (and other) packages are available via conda.

I would suggest that we understand the kinds of file or directory permissions that allow **unlinking** of a package but that will block the subsequent **linking** that needs to happen right afterwards.  And then we need to check for those conditions and not attempt a package update if those conditions occur.  If this is really computationally burdensome, then special case `conda` and `python` (in fact, as I discovered, `requests` and a few others might also be required), and just check those.  But destroying Anaconda is a really bad experience.  With Anaconda being increasingly adopted by admins and installed as the `root` user on hosts then with some subsequent `chown` and `chmod` operations, other people will also setup the scenario (as we have with Wakari) where they can more than shoot themselves in the foot, but, in fact, shoot themselves in the head.

I'm not sure that `--force` is enough. That guarantees re-extraction, but not re-fetch. We will have to determine the proper external `conda` behavior is to ensure re-fetching, adding something if needed. Clearing the package cache might be the most expedient choice until then.

> Given that conda is an alternate package manager (whose packages will surely conflict with the ones provided by Debian), I really doubt Debian maintainers will accept it in their repos.

I don't think that being an alternate package manager is by itself a reason not to be included in Debian. (eg. [flatpak](https://packages.debian.org/flatpak) is shipped in Debian, as are tools like [alien](https://packages.debian.org/alien) for converting between packaging formats and various rpm tools). Also, browsers are their package managers these days, and while there are efforts to package addons so they can be installed system-wide with well-understood update policies, and programs are discouraged to give away users starting them by asking for online updates of addons without user confirmation, they are being shipped in Debian.

> For instance, would they be OK with conda installing its own Python, rather than using the Debian, etc. Python? Debian has a huge document of requirements.

Those are mainly on how the package installs itself into the system (eg. "no files in those areas", "when can you use statically linked libraries"), how it behaves during build (eg. "no network access") and how it's licensed (eg. be free software). What it does when it is invoked during regular operation is much less regulated, and as I understand conda so far, it could use the system's Python for running itself, and if it then installs a different Python in a directory given by a user on their behalf, that's fine.

@kalefranz, if you start working on debs, please announce your Intent To Package by opening an issue in Debian's [WNPP](https://www.debian.org/devel/wnpp/) bug tracker (and ideally crossreference with this issue). This will ensure that people interested in packaging Conda for Debian will find it from the other side too.

This error means that you didn't install cpymad for the python distribution you use from your terminal.
Please make sure, you do not confuse different python executables and operating system.

You should build, install and import cpymad in exactly the same circumstances (machine, operating system, python binary).

Maybe its best to test cpymad in an isolated environment, so you don't confuse your python binaries by accident. Do that as follows:

```bash
# install virtualenv (if not installed already)
sudo pip install virtualenv

# create an isolated python environment that has access to packages installed for the system
virtualenv --system-site-packages ENV

# activate virtualenv, do this each time before using cpymad:
source ENV/bin/activate

# go to cpymad folder and then build and install:
python setup.py install

# check if it works
python -c 'import cpymad.libmadx'
```

atmospheric water vapor and aerosol content**, P. Ineichecn, Solar Energy 82 (2008), pp1095-1097][1]

[1]: http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.534.315&rep=rep1&type=pdf

[Ineichen_SolarEnergy(82)_Conversion-of-Linke-turbidity_2008.pdf](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/files/621265/Ineichen_SolarEnergy.82._Conversion-of-Linke-turbidity_2008.pdf)

![Linke turbidity](http://latex.codecogs.com/png.latex?T_{L2}\left(aod_{550},w,p/p_0\right)=3.91\exp{\left(0.689p_0/p\right)}aod_{550}+0.376\ln{\left(w\right)}+\left[2+0.54\left(p_0/p\right)-0.5\left(p_0/p\right)^2+0.16\left(p_0/p\right)^3\right])

>where p is the atmospheric pressure at the considered altitude,
po the sea level atmospheric pressure and aod550 the
aerosol optical depth at 550 nm

Briefly, yes there are standard ways but they are not coded in pvlib.

As background, a module performance model requires certain parameter values (e.g., Rshunt for the CEC model) that are not found on datasheets.  For modules, there are techniques to obtain parameters for some module performance model (e.g., De Soto, CEC) from datasheet values.  The SAM SDK includes these functions, so you may be able to use SAM to get a set of module model parameters. 

For inverters, it's a bit more difficult.  The efficiency curves are needed; these could be digitized from the figure commonly found on an inverter cutsheet. The parameters for the Sandia inverter model can then be estimated by a fitting method.  The method is not difficult (I have a spreadsheet somewhere) and I believe it is also coded in SAM.

Estimation of model parameters from data is an important gap which pvlib currently lacks.  We welcome pull requests :)

Yes.  if averaging by sample, sample=site (by our MagIG definiation)
and if averaging by site, one would average by sample first.  it is confusing because the “site” is what you average over.  the sample is only relavant for oriented ones (in my opinion).  
2 different scenarios:  
1) a lava flow (site) with multiple oriented cores (samples) that have been chopped into multiple slices to measure (specimens).   If we are after a direction and orientation is the key source of uncertainty, then we would average multiple specimens by sample, then by site.  this is what most paleomagnetists do.  
2) we have a bunch of pot sherds from an archaeological level.  one might have initatially designated each level a “site” but the only thing we are really  sure got cooled under the same conditions are pieces from the same pot.  the pieces would be ‘samples’. if they can be tied together into a single pot, then the pot would be the “site”. but mostly we just get individual sherds that are unrelated.  here we average intensities by ‘sample” (sherd) and that is actually the ‘site’ by MagIC definitions.  At anyrate, the sample averages belong at the site level here.  site info entered as above must be preserved however. Ideally, if the samples were originally grouped into ‘sites’ but these are no longer appropriate because the ‘site’ mixed up things of different ages, then new site names must be invented that have all the metadata from the old site (geospatial info like lat/long/square/level/inferred age, etc.).

> However I think we can agree that fact that exceptions raised in setup methods prevents tear down method from run really sucks. I cannot imagine what was the reason to that in such impractical way. However I'm not unittest expert, so it's possible that deep in internals of unittest (and also nosetest) is good reason for that.

I understand where you're coming from, but I disagree. I suggest reading [Perhaps unittest is wrong, and tearDown should always run](http://pythontesting.net/framework/unittest/unittest-wrong-teardown/) and the comments at the bottom of the page. The first comment by masklinn gets at the heart of the issue:

> I don’t think you’ve thought this through.
>
> Consider your own example: setup is supposed to create a directory, open a connection and set up some other stuff. setup blows up and throws, you try to run teardown anyway.
>
> And it blows up because it tries to del an attribute which was never created in the first place.
>
> If your setup blows up, there is no guarantee about its partial state, and the teardown simply can’t be run. It’s the same reason why __exit__ is *not* run if __enter__ throws.
>
> For what you want, you need to build a stack of setup/teardown operations (similar to exitStack), but setup and teardown are not up to the task

The comments include a variety of suggestions for solving the issue.

@jenshnielsen as far as I can judge it as a newbie in qcodes is this node feature by @AdriaanRol complementary to the existing channels in the sense that while channels are great for homogeneous parameter structures, nodes are great for inhomogeneous parameters. By this I mean that a channel offers a lot of comfort for e.g. an oscilloscope with four channels that all behave in the same way. For the implementation of an instrument with "channels" that share no structure one would have to chose between creating specialized channel classes or to have all the parameters living at the root level.
I think the nodes concept could offer a great third option, that allows for the structuring of the parameters without the need for creating all the nested channel objects.

I've tested the case that calls `.asnumpy()` before using the numpy.sum. However, the numpy version is still faster:
```python
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
import numpy as np
import time

# Warm up the GPU
for _ in range(10):
    a = nd.ones((100, 100), ctx=mx.gpu())
    b = a * 2
    b.asnumpy()

N = 100

# Test the speed
for data_shape in [(16,), (64,), (256,), (1024,)]:
    dat_npy = np.random.uniform(0, 1, data_shape)
    dat_nd_gpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.gpu())
    dat_nd_cpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.cpu())
    nd.waitall()
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        np_ret = np.sum(dat_nd_gpu.asnumpy())
    end = time.time()
    np_copy_from_gpu_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        np_ret = np.sum(dat_nd_cpu.asnumpy())
    end = time.time()
    np_copy_from_cpu_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_gpu).asscalar()
    end = time.time()
    nd_gpu_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_cpu).asscalar()
    end = time.time()
    nd_cpu_time = end - start
    print('sum, data_shape=%s, numpy from gpu=%g, numpy from cpu=%g, mxnet gpu=%g, mxnet cpu=%g' %(str(data_shape), np_copy_from_gpu_time, np_copy_from_cpu_time, nd_gpu_time, nd_cpu_time))
```

> Are you running on an EC2 with an IAM role attached that has a policy that allows writing to the S3 bucket?

> In the docs there's a list of 8 places boto3 looks for credentials. #6 is an explicit assume role provider, number 8 is EC2 instance IAM metadata.

No, I'm running Airflow locally in a docker container and use assumerole to access resources within an AWS account. I'll provide some context, sorry for the wall of text.

The fact that I'm running Airflow in a container makes the regular way of using assumerole (adding a profile to `~/.aws/config` and passing that profile's name to `boto3.client()`) pretty impractical/impossible because I can't properly volume mount my `~/.aws/config`. So Airflow's option to use assumerole by setting the role-arn was very welcome.
For people who want to use assumerole the regular way it would be enough to allow the user to pass the profile name.
Note that that won't solve my usecase and the usecase where Airflow is run in AWS account 1 and wants to access resources in another AWS account using assumerole. That would require the solution as it was previously with specifying the role to assume and doing an explicit assumerole call.

Anyway, the fallback to boto3 credentials is working, I did the assumerole call myself using AWS cli and set the `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` and `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` env vars based on that. After that logging to S3 worked using these credentials.
Doing this is not ideal because I have to do the assumerole call myself and then convert the response from AWS to env vars and the credentials time out whereas if you leave the assumerole action to boto3 it gets renewed automatically.

How would user specify which behavior is needed if they are mixed together? Reboot and disconnect require different verification, like in disconnect, error is thrown if snippet crashed during disconnect, so users need to explicitly specify which behavior is intended by using different methods. So it's better to have separate calls than `if` statements everywhere in the code path. It's easier to use, test, and maintain. Update on `pyinstaller`: It works, sorta. Instead of using `click_plugins.with_plugins` to dynamically pull the subcommands from the entry points, you can explictly define subcommand as part of a group

```diff
+from .cli  import main_group

-@click.command(short_help="Geocode an address or coordinates.")
+@main_group.command(short_help="Geocode an address or coordinates.")
```

write the main `cli.py` as a wrapper script to do explicit imports

```
from mapboxcli.scripts.geocoding import geocoding

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main_group()
```

then run pyinstaller on the wrapper script to produce an executable

```
$ file dist/mapbox/mapbox
mapbox: Mach-O 64-bit executable x86_64
```
and geocoding works as expected. 

Implications of this approach? Compiled script means no dynamic plugins at runtime, lose the ability to do user plugins.

Sorry for the delay in response. Okay first off @gamaanderson can you tell me which is the "old" and "new"? The first plot above looks more like the airborne radar I would expect.

To comment on the structure of the program. There is some back story (as always) with airborne radar data. Before about 2012, the data were saved differently, in blocks of code containing ray data for a block of time. A lot of hardware changes were made at that time and after much discussion, we decided that saving one *sweep* might be the best way forward. However, many legacy programs deal with data on a time period basis. This mean that a number of sweeps - or more properly a continuously rotating antenna would form a sort of conical volume. Remember that this scanning is intrinsically different than ground-based radar as the platform is moving at non-negligilbe speed. New methods were not (and I would argue still ARE not developed) and these data *look* different than you would expect from a nice stationary ground system. 

I have not dove back in to the data recently. But will try to speak with some people soon about this.
The data go through more geolocation steps due to movement. In addition, aircraft sampling angles constantly change (turns, turbulence, etc.). So the data turns out to be more complicated than the average radar in geometric terms.

IF plans continue as expected a second antenna will be added to this type of radar system. Other airborne platforms (not active scanners) already have up to 5 potential antennas. This complicates matters well beyond anything PyART can currently handle and I don't have a clue at the moment how to handle this. So the airborne class may become outdated.

> I know there is a recent pep on versioning, could we add a sufix like 0.7.0.devN, where we increment N when we add new interface?

Yes, except that you'd use `0.7.1.devN` (all the `.dev` come before the release, i.e., `0.7.0.dev1` would be older than `0.7.0` according to [PEP 440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#examples-of-compliant-version-schemes)).

If you did that, we could append `#egg=Theano==0.7.1.devN` with the correct version number to the git reference in our `requirements.txt`, and we could check for the correct version by the version string rather than by a feature test.

> Where does the embedding concept come from?

You're defining a network that produces a vector for each input example. The network is then trained to increase or decrease the euclidean distance (called `d` in the opening post) for pairs of such vectors (called `a` and `b` in the opening post) depending on whether they belong to the same class or not.

Since the network produces vectors, and the vectors are compared by Euclidean distance, the network learns an embedding of objects into a Euclidean space where you can (hopefully) compare objects in a meaningful way -- similar to how a word embedding produces vectors for words to make it easier to compare them in a meaningful way.

> Why does the linear layer make more sense?

Because it allows the network to use the full, untransformed output space. If you place a nonlinearity in front of the output, the vectors will be squashed (for a sigmoid function) or crippled (for a rectifier), and the Euclidean distance doesn't really fit any more.

Here's @yungchin's [initial explanation](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/pull/344#issuecomment-157762655) of why the delivery report queues are thread local:

> The main reason I started out with a thread-local queue here is that it nicely supports a use model that the futures interface also supported well: if you have something like a multi-threaded web server, with all threads sharing a single producer, but each thread checking its own delivery failures (or each running with sync=True), then you don't want them to de-queue each others reports. On the other hand, I suppose there may be use models where you'd produce() from multiple threads but have a single thread somewhere handling all delivery failures.

It looks like this may be a [case](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/pull/344#issuecomment-157850921) for rethinking this design.

Have you considered creating an exception monitoring thread per producing thread and  putting the caught exceptions onto a queue?

The [kafka documentation](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-ErrorCodes) implies that the total size of a batch has no bearing on whether `MessageSizeTooLarge` is returned:

> The server has a configurable maximum message size to avoid unbounded memory allocation. This error is thrown if the client attempt to produce a message larger than this maximum.

This undoes parts of #2262 and #3383 (but leaves the parts about fixing the `ssl_session_cache`, for example).

We previously thought that it would be easier to upgrade ciphersuites if we kept them inline, but it turns out that's not the case.

Yes, this runs the risk of accidentally duplicating specific directives in a single server block. But, that's arguably safer now than it was when we made the original change. First, we only ever do this to non-ssl server blocks, and we more robustly detect which are and aren't server blocks now. So the only case where it's a problem is when there are already ssl directives in a non-ssl server block, at which point something else has probably also already gone even more terribly wrong.

Some people who used `/etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-nginx.conf` in the past might have manually modified it, because it lives in `etc`. We should make sure that old versions are kept around when we add the updating code (which isn't in here just yet, and should probably just go in a separate PR). As a bonus for adding that, people who have the super-old version (from before we changed to `ssl_session_cache shared:le_nginx_SSL:1m;`) will get the update to fix that for free!

Did you have any closed indices? I think I know what the problem was.  

[Curator already checks for an empty list condition](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.4.1/curator/actions.py#L801) before proceeding.  The problem is that it [checks for closed indices _after_ that](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.4.1/curator/actions.py#L802-L806).  I'm changing the order of these statement so an empty list cannot be passed here.

You do that but creating a mapping via the `indices.put_mapping` API or when creating the index via `indices.create`.

If you want a more convenient way to create mappings from python you should check out the DSL library where this could be done by doing:

    from elasticsearch_dsl import DocType, Nested, String, GeopPoint

    class MyType(DocType):
        name = String()
        nationality = String()

        address = Nested(
            properties={
                'location': GeoPoint(),
                'state': String(),
                'city': String(),
            }
        )


Then you can just create the index which will put the mappings into elasticsearch:


    from elasticsearch_dsl import Index

    myindex = Index('myindex')
    myindex.doc_type(MyType)
    myindex.create()

and you can use it. See http://elasticsearch-dsl.readthedocs.org/en/latest/persistence.html for more details.

To play Devil's advocate - we almost always specify the seeds explicitly when instantiating the algorithms so we would still have deterministic tests, ability to spot platform differences, etc.

One advantage of having the default be the special value "-1" is that we can introduce a function to change the default across the board. By default, "-1" would choose a random seed but you could call `Random.setDefaultSeed(42)` and any time an algorithm instantiates `Random(-1)` after that it uses the seed "42" rather than a random seed.

Again, just playing devil's advocate.

If you wanted to try other types of neighborhood, e.g. using the Manhattan Distance or Birds Eye Distance rather than a simple hypercube, you'd create a new iterator class (sort of like how I created a new one for `WrappingNeighborhood`).

If we ever build an extensibility point for topology, I think we'd choose a Neighborhood class via a factory or a simple virtual method on the SP. The instance would have to be stored on the heap since polymorphism only works with pointers/references. I doubt we could use this iterator syntax, since iterators are typically used by value, not by pointer/reference, and polymorphism would require them to be a pointer to a `Base::Iterator`. So we'd have to change the interface to the class, maybe giving it `next()` and `isFinished()` methods.

(Note that before this change topology was not extensible via subclassing -- you can't override `_getNeighborsND` since it's not virtual)

Thanks for reporting the problem. The main issue is that we built these wheels using a version of Python that was linked to a shared library. We recently fixed this issue in Linux by using a manylinux docker image that has Python preinstalled in such a way that there is no shared library.

We have not done the same with OSX yet, because we need to setup a travis script that properly installs each version of Python from source (or find a prebuilt solution that fits this no shared library requirement).

This will take some time to fix, but we do plan a fix. I will leave this ticket open until we do.

Also, is there a big need for Python 3.4 or 3.5 on a Mac? I am not that familiar with Python 3.x. I was planning on dropping support for 3.4 and 3.5 on Mac and Windows (keeping it on Linux is trivial with the docker image we are using).

Best,
Rob

I'm slightly shocked by this; until I read this issue I had always assumed factories where called once, not for every request. I never even thought to check.

If they're called for every request, what's the point in having the outer coroutine?

I've just looked now at a project I'm working on, almost every middleware has processing outside the "handler" which I had assumed would only be called once. None of this processing will take very long so when testing or deploying with small or moderate usage it wouldn't be obvious, but if performance becomes important this could have been a problem.

Apparently this surprising behaviour isn't [documented](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web.html#middlewares). (or am I being blind?)

Solutions (in order of my preference):
1. call middleware factories once when freezing the app.
2. remove the outer coroutine so middlewares are just simple coroutines.
3. add a big fat prominent warning to the docs that the behaviour of middleware is not intuitive. 

I'm guessing 1) is too big a change to be accepted? (My guess would be, 90%+ of developers assume this behaviour anyway, so it shouldn't cause problems)

Perhaps 2) would be possible if current behaviour was allowed but just raises a depreciation warning for a few releases?

Just a couple of comments to try to put all of us in the same page. This issue is about the tracing in the server side, to track those news connections and requests that flows into the server. For the Client side perspective, Aiohttp already has an implementation of the Client tracing since 3.0 [1].

So, first of all. This is about the server or the client side?

Regarding the Client side, current implementation allows passing static and dynamic configurations, maybe the solution is a bit overcomplicated but you should be able to make both things in your trace configs. The usage of one pattern or another was on the air at the moment of developing this feature, contextvars were discarded because Aiohttp gives support for Python versions > 3.5.

So the current implementation does allow to pass contextual information per request using the `trace_request_ctx`, but the developer has the freedom of using other mechanisms such as contextvars, TaskLocal, aiocontext, .... that will circumvent the current implementation and will allow pass information implicitly between the caller and the signals.

OK, so you're talking about `watchman-make`.  Neither `trigger` nor `watchman-make` kill off the running process.  This is by design; we don't know that it would leave things in a reasonable state if we opted to kill things off.  In addition, many build processes do things like generate sources that are likely to match your build invocation trigger, so if we killed things off as files changed, the build would not make progress.

Both `trigger` and `watchman-make` are intended to be quick and easy solutions to spawning (relatively) short lived processes as files change.  If you have a long lived build process that you'd like to terminate early when certain files change, then the most robust thing to do is have that process establish a watchman subscription and use the information from that subscription to terminate at safe points in the build process.

Since you're using nodejs, this documentation is the most relevant for establishing subscriptions:
https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/nodejs.html

One issue we have with this is that `create` already [takes a list as the `path`](https://github.com/fatiando/pooch/blob/master/pooch/core.py#L34) but it joins them automatically. This was to spare the user from having to import `os.path` but now it comes back to bite us (designing APIs is **hard**). Since we only have 2 users right now, I don't it would be bad to break this. 

I think these changes to the API would solve this problem:

* `os_cache` returns a list of directories. We still need to determine what this list should be but something like `[default cache dir, user dir]`. 
* `create` will not be able to join paths anymore.
* `create` can take a list of directories and test each one in order.

But now that I think about, in your example the user directory is `/usr/share/httpd` for some reason. So no matter what we chose in `os_cache`, we wouldn't have permission to write to it. So we should probably add a `try: .. except:` block to add a message telling the user to set the environment variable if `os.makedirs` fails. I don't if there is much else we can do.

This also occurs when numbers become `Decimal`, something [commonly done when parsing json](http://www.daveoncode.com/2014/10/21/python-reading-numbers-from-json-without-loss-of-precision-using-decimal-class-for-data-processing/) that may have been generated by an implementation with a different float precision than python.  E.g.:

```
>>> jmespath.search('[?b > `2`]', [{"a": 1, "b": 2}, {"a": 1, "b": Decimal(3)}])
[]
```

It seems that to deal with both cases, the change implemented [here](https://github.com/jmespath/jmespath.py/commit/6f2f5834b5df755266ff3f7a5fecb05a019d6da0#diff-e264d131fa420ca3ff588c5a9ce58c75R46) would have been better written to use `Number`:

```
from numbers import Number
def _is_actual_number(x):
     # We need to handle python's quirkiness with booleans,
     # specifically:
     #
     # >>> isinstance(False, int)
     # True
     # >>> isinstance(True, int)
     # True
     if x is True or x is False:
         return False
     return isinstance(x, Number)
```

Here is an excerpt from RFC6242:

4.1.  Framing Protocol

   The previous version of this document defined the character sequence
   "]]>]]>" as a message separator, under the assumption that it could
   not be found in well-formed XML documents.  However, this assumption
   is not correct.  It can legally appear in XML attributes, comments,
   and processing instructions.  In order to solve this problem, and at
   the same time be compatible with existing implementations, this
   document defines the following framing protocol.

   The <hello> message MUST be followed by the character sequence
   ]]>]]>.  Upon reception of the <hello> message, the receiving peer's
   SSH Transport layer conceptually passes the <hello> message to the
   Messages layer.  **If the :base:1.1 capability is advertised by both
   peers, the chunked framing mechanism (see Section 4.2) is used for
   the remainder of the NETCONF session.**  Otherwise, the old end-of-
   message-based mechanism (see Section 4.3) is used.

```
db = Database('my_db', autocreate=False)
```
