I find this slightly ambiguous: as a possible reviewer, when seeing this label, am I supposed to review or is someone already doing it?

We do it with assignments: when an implementor has finished work, s·he unassigns herself.
If a PR is open with no assignee, then it is waiting for review.
Once a reviewer starts work, s·he assigns herself.
Once review is done, reviewer unassigns herself and reassigns the original implementor.
The original implementor can then work, and this cycles until a GTM is obtained, at which point the original implementor is expected to merge her work.

No need for a label to request reviews since everything is expected to be reviewed, no need for a label that says a review is in progress because for efficiency reviews should not be parallel.

With only a marginal understanding, I have produced this monkey patch which has gotten me past the above and a subsequent ValueError:

```python
# API server is returning invalid values according to the demands
# of the swagger generated API client. This monkeypatch works
# around the bugs I have run into so far.
#
# Related issues:
# - https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/issues/415
# - https://github.com/openshift/openshift-restclient-python/issues/139
# - https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/issues/418
# - https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/issues/394

# tripped by openshift.client.OapiApi.list_namespaced_role_binding()
from kubernetes.client.models.v1_role_ref import V1RoleRef

@property
def kind(self):
    return self._kind

@kind.setter
def kind(self, kind):
    if kind is None:
        kind = ''
    self._kind = kind

@property
def api_group(self):
    return self._api_group

@api_group.setter
def api_group(self, api_group):
    if api_group is None:
        api_group = ''
    self._api_group = api_group

V1RoleRef.kind = kind
V1RoleRef.api_group = api_group 
```

After some more reading, I'm dubious about the design of HashClient. The [memcached docs recommend](https://github.com/memcached/memcached/wiki/ConfiguringClient#failure-or-failover):

> In the dark days of memcached, the default was to always "failover", by trying the next server in the list. That way if a server crashes, its keys will get reassigned to other instances and everything moves on happily. ... Modern life encourages the use of "Failure", when possible. That is, if the server you intend to fetch or store a cache entry to is unavailable, simply proceed as though it was a cache miss.

In my case, I've decided to not bother with HashClient. I'm just going to wrap instances of PooledClient which are chosen using RendezvousHash and not bother with retry logic, adding/removing servers, etc. KISS and all that. I'll revisit this issue when I'm done with that. Minimally, I'll submit a PR updating the docs about thread-safety and this library.

In any case, thanks you for this library I chose it after reading over the code and the base Client is solid.

# Spec

This spec covers the minimal requirements for an initial working version of blurr.

## Application Bootstrap

App has a CLI (Command Line Interface) that can be installed via `pip`:
```
pip install blurr
```
Provided the appropriate AWS credentials are configured in the host account, a user can run the `deploy` command:
```
blurr deploy
```

## Configuration

App configuration is set via command line options. The following events are available:
- `--stack-name`: the name of the Cloudformation Stack where blurr is deployed. _[Optional: default=blurr]_
- `--source-s3-bucket`: URI of the s3 bucket blurr subscribes to. If not defined, blurr will create a new one.  _[Optional: default=blurr-events]_

Example:
```
blurr deploy --stack-name my-blurr-stack --source-s3-bucket s3://app-logs
```

Configuration parameters are printed using the `config` option, providing the name of the stack:
```
blurr config --stack-name=my-blurr-stack
  source-s3-buckets:
    - s3://app-logs
    - s3://different-app-logs
```

## Configuration Update

Some of the configuration parameters are immutable and can't be updated after a blurr stack is deployed.  Other parameters can be set or updated in a running stack.

To apply a configuration update in a running stack we must provide the name of the stack we desire to update:
```
blurr config source-set --stack-name my-blurr-stack --source-s3-bucket s3://different-app-logs
```

This is a list of available configuration update commands
```
# replaces the s3 bucket blurr is subscribed to
blurr config source-set --stack-name my-blurr-stack --source-s3-bucket s3://different-app-logs

# adds a new s3 bucket subscription
blurr config source-add --stack-name my-blurr-stack --source-s3-bucket s3://external-logs

# deletes a subscribtion to a  s3 bucket
blurr config source-delete --stack-name my-blurr-stack --source-s3-bucket s3://external-logs
```

# Implementation Overview

- The app is a standard `pip` CLI app
- The `deploy` command runs a set of Cloudformation commands, providing the appropiate output information.
- Internally, each configuration update executes a lambda function that performs the configuration update

Ok, to work on GAE, you need to monkeypatch it like this:

```python
import analytics.client
import analytics.request
_original_analytics_enqueue = analytics.client.Client._enqueue
def _monkeypatched_analytics_enqueue(client, message):
    if client.queue.put != analytics.client.queue.Queue.put:
        def monkeypatched_analytics_queue_put(msg, block):
            analytics.request.post(constants.SEGMENT_KEY, batch=[msg])
        client.queue.put = monkeypatched_analytics_queue_put

    if client.consumer.running:
        client.consumer.running = False

    _original_analytics_enqueue(client, message)
analytics.client.Client._enqueue = _monkeypatched_analytics_enqueue
```

This forces all the messages to be sent synchronously and stops any running extra threads to prevent GAE from locking up by waiting on those threads.

```haskell
{-# LANGUAGE OverloadedStrings #-}
{-# LANGUAGE RecordWildCards #-}

module Msg
  ( loop
  , receive
  , toCrc16 ) where

import           CRC16                    ( crc16 )
import           Trace                    ( trace )
import           Types

import           System.IO.Streams        ( InputStream, OutputStream )
import           System.IO.Streams.Binary ( binaryFromStream )

import           Control.Monad            ( when )

import           Data.Binary
import           Data.Binary.Get
import           Data.Binary.Put
import           Data.ByteString          ( ByteString )
import qualified Data.ByteString as BS    ( length )
import           Data.ByteString.Builder
import           Data.Monoid              ( mconcat )

import           Data.Foldable            ( forM_ )
import           Data.Traversable         ( forM )

import           Text.Printf              ( printf )

msgPreamble :: Word8
msgPreamble = 0x55

instance Binary Msg where
  put Msg {..} = do
    putWord8 msgPreamble
    putWord16le msgType
    putWord16le msgSender
    putWord8 msgLen
    putByteString msgPayload
    putWord16le msgCrc

  get = do
    preamble <- getWord8
    if preamble /= msgPreamble
      then
        get
      else do
        msgType <- getWord16le
        msgSender <- getWord16le
        msgLen <- getWord8
        msgPayload <- getByteString $ fromIntegral msgLen
        msgCrc <- getWord16le
        return Msg {..}

mkMsg :: Word16 -> Word16 -> ByteString -> Msg
mkMsg msgType' msgSender' msgPayload' = go Msg
  { msgType    = msgType'
  , msgSender  = msgSender'
  , msgLen     = fromIntegral $ BS.length msgPayload'
  , msgPayload = msgPayload'
  , msgCrc     = 0 } where
    go msg@Msg {..} = Msg
      { msgCrc = toCrc16 msg
      , ..}

toBuilder :: Msg -> Builder
toBuilder Msg {..} = mconcat
  [ word16LE msgType
  , word16LE msgSender
  , word8 msgLen
  , byteString msgPayload ]

toCrc16 :: Msg -> Word16
toCrc16 = crc16 . toLazyByteString . toBuilder

receive :: InputStream ByteString -> IO (Maybe Msg)
receive readBuf' = do
  trace "event=receive"
  msg <- binaryFromStream readBuf'
  forM msg $ \m -> do
    when (msgCrc m /= toCrc16 m) $
      trace $ printf "event=bad-crc msg-crc=%x crc=%x" (msgCrc m) (toCrc16 m)
    return m

loop :: InputStream ByteString -> Handler -> IO ()
loop readBuf' handler' = do
  trace "event=loop"
  go where
    go = do
      msg <- receive readBuf'
      forM_ msg $ \m -> do
        handler' m
        go
```

> Mostly, I wanted to understand our options and where autobahn-python is headed to make sure that we're best suited for handling increasing loads.

Here is the short story:

1. scaling up (making use of multiple cores) and out (making use of multiple machines) of  **app components**: we consider this mostly solved in CB already: https://github.com/crossbario/crossbar-examples/tree/master/scaling-microservices
2. scaling up/out the CB routing core itself: this will come (as part of Crossbar.io Fabric) in two steps: a) introducing a new proxy worker type and b) adding router-to-router messaging
3. the first line of load-balancing (towards the proxy workers) is either coming from one or more L4 LBs or directly from DNS (round-robin/HA).

Now, the communication between proxy workers and router workers, and between different router workers will need to transport many sessions.

And here, transport many session about one underlying TCP is where I currently think this feature (many sessions over one transport) make most sense, and can be of real benefit.

However, due to the specific additional requirements for communication between proxy-router and router-router, this feature could end up not being part of AB, but CB Fabric only.

> where we ran into issues with having too many open connections

Systems that spawn one OS process per incoming client connection will run into issues with large connections numbers obviously. Eg this applies to PostgreSQL. To counter that, there are things like PL/Bounder and PL/Proxy.

Crossbar.io doesn't have that issue. I have tested CB on my notebook with 200k connections. No problem. Of course if those 200k connections will do a lot of messaging, then this will be the limit (CB on a single Xeon core can route calls at roughly 50k routed calls per second).

The messaging rate it much more interesting as a limit for CB rather than connection count.

Given enough RAM and a decent NIC and kernel, you should be able to keep 1M mostly idle connections on a single box.

However, with 1M connections, and only doing WebSocket ping/pong for heartbeating at say once every 10s (eg http://crossbar.io/docs/WebSocket-Options/#production-settings), this already means 100k pings sent and 100k pongs received per second.

Anyways, hope those lines shed some light on the topic ..

Why are you referring to `_data_dict` throughout this PR? The whole point of Python `@property` methods is that it's a seamless interface. I didn't introduce `_data_dict` to be "the new `_data`", it's there as an internal implementation detail behind the `_data` decorator so that we can perform actions when it is set. The *only* place `_data_dict` should be referenced is inside `def _data()` and to set its initial value in `__init__()`.

I've implemented this as it required a change of the Schema so I think getting the Schema sorted sooner rather than later is better than convincing people to do some sort of update later on for a new feature.

I've thought about this a little bit - I'm not sure there's a really a win in adding more code to the constructor to achieve this. 

The reason we ended up with:

```python
        req = pubsub_pb2.Topic(name=name)
        settings = self._defaults['create_topic'].merge(options)
        api_call = api_callable.create_api_call(self.stub.CreateTopic,
                                                settings=settings)
        return api_call(req, metadata=self._headers)
```

and not something shorter (which we had before) is because someone reading it can make sense of what it's doing without having to refer to other code.

```python
        req = pubsub_pb2.Topic(name=name)
        return self._create_topic_callable(req, options)
```

An earlier iteration of the generated python code looked a bit like this, i.e we initially aimed for this kind of terseness.  The feedback we received was 'what does this mean?  what is _create_topic_callable doing ?'

In the end, we switched to the slightly longer version we have now.  Arguably it is easier to understand for someone browsing the code.

About the parser error issue with the custom userData, the issue should go away if you wrap the string value with quotes `com.adobe.type.autohint = "<ICA8aGludFNldExp..>"`.

The glyphsLib parser accepts any character if it is between double quotes (the quote character iteself has to be escaped with backslash); whereas if it is outside of quotes, it only accepts characters `[-_./$A-Za-z0-9]+`.
Here is the regex used:
https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/700d96cd9c546c20208606123f34806bb6156a79/Lib/glyphsLib/parser.py#L29

This was indirectly fixed by 47e9140e, specifically this change:

```diff
diff --git a/src/python/pants/ivy/ivy.py b/src/python/pants/ivy/ivy.py
index f36af27..0b67d23 100644
--- a/src/python/pants/ivy/ivy.py
+++ b/src/python/pants/ivy/ivy.py
   def runner(self, jvm_options=None, args=None, executor=None):
     """Creates an ivy commandline client runner for the given args."""
     args = args or []
-    executor = executor or self._java
+    executor = executor or SubprocessExecutor()
     if not isinstance(executor, Executor):
       raise ValueError('The executor argument must be an Executor instance, given %s of type %s'
                        % (executor, type(executor)))
```

The issue was that `executor` would previously fall back on `self._java`, which was also `None`, and then `executor.Error` would fail with the above stack trace.  Now it falls back on `SubprocessExecutor()` which always succeeds.

> We have some flexibility here, as we haven’t designed the audio tensor format yet. The current summary includes a bunch of global metadata: the sample rate, channel count, number of samples, and MIME type. All of this is junk: the only field among these that’s even used is the content type, but that is always audio/wav.[1] So, if we remove all these, then the tensor would look like a rank-1 string tensor of [wav_data_0, wav_data_1, …]. Perhaps we could instead use a rank-2 string tensor of the form [[wav_data_0, wav_data_1, …], [markdown_metadata_0, markdown_metadata_1, …]], which would allow you to provide a transcript or any other information that you like. This could easily come from a string tensor.

Yea, I'm the original author of audio summary support in TF/TB, so this is all my fault :). 

WAV is indeed a huge waste of space -- my team alone is spending many terabytes of space on storing our events files because of all the WAVs we produce across all our experiments (and I know audio summaries are a bit of a storage pain for a certain "ML dashboard")

I left mimetype in there as room for expansion basically, so we could eventually include a proper lossy or lossless codec at some point for compression (e.g. FLAC). Adding a dependency like libFLAC to TensorFlow was much harder than whipping up a WAV encoder by hand, so I went with that originally. 

Note tf.contrib.ffmpeg is an optional dependency, so you can't rely on FFMPEG being available (which would make this a lot easier, though security-wise it's not allowed for use internally without sandboxing).

As for codec, a lossless codec would be preferred (e.g. FLAC over Vorbis) so we know distortions in the audio came from our model, not the codec.

Hmm, this sort of addresses #830 but I am not sure it's really a complete solution. The logic here only escapes the HTML output from the markdown processing (which happens server-side), so it shows up as literal HTML text, tags and all. This does mean that if someone's generating text that might include HTML tags, this would keep some of those tags from getting interpreted (because various HTML tags like `` pass through the markdown interpretation). But it doesn't fix the issues caused by the markdown interpretation itself inserting new tags. To illustrate this, we can look at three levels of text interpretation: 1. Some *text*. 2. `Some text.` 3. `Some *text*.` This PR gets us from 1 to 2, but I think the real goal should be getting to 3, which shows the actual raw text summary output. Note that this is harder though, since the markdown interpretation is done on the server and can't just be skipped from the browser without plumbing through a toggle. I actually wonder whether it might be better to just move the markdown rendering to the browser-side. It makes the sanitization story a little simpler since then we don't have to just "trust" output from the server to be sanitized; we can ensure that it's sanitized as late as possible before insertion into the DOM. But we might have trouble finding a sufficiently good sanitizer. The [markdown element](https://www.webcomponents.org/element/PolymerElements/marked-element) that comes with polymer uses [marked.js](https://github.com/chjj/marked) but the sanitization logic it uses seems fairly XSS-prone.

@zeynali Hi there, Could you please update the status of this issue and share if you managed to solve it. many thanks

RPCs have a max payload size of `4194304` bytes (by default). Your program is raising an exception when debugger logic (within `TensorBoardDebugWrapperSession`) tries to [send the graph of the model to TensorBoard](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/7cfa54d24ad311a42f596eb98948d51a4aa057c0/tensorflow/python/debug/wrappers/grpc_wrapper.py#L223). Perhaps the graph is large.

For now, would it be OK for you to prevent the debugger from sending the graph (and python tracebacks) to TensorBoard? Via setting `end_traceback_and_source_code` to `False`:

```python
sess = tf_debug.TensorBoardDebugWrapperSession(
    sess,
    'localhost:6064',
    send_traceback_and_source_code=False)
```

You would not be able to use the debugger plugin's source code view, which maps tensor values to lines of python code. However, the graph will still show in TensorBoard (because it's provided elsewhere aside from via RPC).

cc @caisq

Ok, so I did some reading on retrieving large data from the database. Basically, there seem to be the following options to alleviate your problem:

* Option 1: Allow turbodbc users to configure a maximum of characters to retrieve, even if the database indicates that this might not be sufficient for all data.
* Option 2: Use the ODBC function `SQLGetData()` to [retrieve long data](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/develop-app/getting-long-data). This seems to have some issues attached, such that it may not be possible to read batches larger than single rows.
* Option 3: Use a reasonably sized (configurable) buffer for results. For each row in a batch, check whether warnings are present for [each fetched row](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/syntax/sqlfetch-function). Refetch rows with warnings attached using larger buffers. This sets higher requirements on the database driver, since it needs to support cursor scrolling. It may also not play well with asynchronous I/O.

Though options 2 and 3 are nice to have, I feel that option 1 is the only one that would really be in line with turbodbc's target audience of data scientists. They typically have to deal with numbers, dates, and strings of limited length. For them, bulk transfer as currently implemented is ideal.

Option 1 would allow to extend this approach to more extensive fields while keeping memory consumption acceptable. The price would be possible truncation of data. I think that is okay for turbodbc's target audience.

Options 2 and 3 could be implemented at a (very) later stage.

For the time being, you could use the following workarounds:
* Do not select long columns.
* When you do select long columns, cast them into types of limited length such as `VARCHAR(1000)`.

Suggest 4096 too.
Security must be good by design and default : no standard user change the default configuration.

And there is no acceptable argument for 2048/3072 vs 4096 bits (only a very small speed overhead).
3072 bits can lead to compatibility problem if user agent hardcode some key sizes (2048 and 4096 bits will be better supported than 3072).

Worst, argument of the 90 days key regeneration to justify 2048 bits key usage is not good :

 * Key regeneration breaks a lot of others TLS stack protection, like DANE/TLSA or HPKP, hardened configuration must disable it to avoid big trouble.
 * Key regeneration doesn’t say "destruction of the past". If the web server is not configured correctly with only PFS ciphers suite, NSA-like agencies can intercep and store network communication protected with 2048 bits key, and can decrypt them in 20 or 30 years, even if the corresponding keys were destroy. 4096 bits keys protect longer non PFS communication.
 * Even with PFS only ciphers, web servers generally base the negociated DH parameter on the private key size. If you use only 2048 bits private key, your DH parameter is 2048 bits only, and so possible weak to LogJam.

It sounds like you have enabled HSTS for your domain. Check your apache configuration for any mention of `Strict-Transport-Security`.

HSTS, unlike a HTTP redirect, causes a browser that has previously visited your site to switch to `https://` for any request, even after you've cleared the browser's cache (which would clear the cached redirect, no matter if 301 or 302).

HTTP 301 seems like the correct option here. 302 could have implications on SEO, among other things.

The other difference to be aware of is that when you're using the queue from trio, then the `trio.Queue` operations integrate with trio's cancellation system, so for example you can wake up a task that's blocked on `trio.Queue.get`. This can be handy sometimes, and may also make your program better behaved if you hit control-C or have an unexpected error. `run_sync_in_worker_thread` is useful but it is one of the only operations in trio that's not cancellable.

> Do you have some newbie tutorial for molecular simulation topics?

1. Justin Lemkul's (@jalemkul) [Gromacs Tutorials](http://www.mdtutorials.com/gmx/), especially the [Tutorial 1: Lysozyme in water](http://www.mdtutorials.com/gmx/lysozyme/index.html). Justin is working on a manuscript describing the tutorials in depth, which is available pre-publication as [gmx_tutorials.pdf](https://github.com/jalemkul/gmx_tutorials_livecoms/blob/master/gmx_tutorials.pdf). We could certainly "GromacsWrapper-ify" the Lysozyme Tutorial and then reference Justin's paper and site.
2.  Our draft of the [AdK tutorial](https://adkgromacstutorial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) (data at [Becksteinlab/AdKGromacsTutorial)](https://github.com/Becksteinlab/AdKGromacsTutorial)) could also be used as a starting point but it has not been fully updated to work correctly with Gromacs 2018 (see the [issues](https://github.com/Becksteinlab/AdKGromacsTutorial/issues)). 

I recommend (1) to learn how to run a simple simulation with Gromacs 2018.

I should point out that the problems with (2) show potential pitfalls with the claim that GromacsWrapper can write "Gromacs version-independent" scripts because input files and options can still change between Gromacs releases. It would be _very_ interesting to write a mini-tutorial that demonstrates how to take care of these differences with minimal impact. (I think we need a function in GromacsWrapper that identifies the Gromacs release... something such `gromacs.gmx_release` so that code can make decisions based on the release).

We could expose a log callback, it is not totally clear how it should be called though.
librdkafka allows for a pluggable log_cb that is currently called from internal librdkafka threads with the contract that the application must not do any prolonged work in its callback - this is a problem in C but nearly impossible with Python due to the overhead (and developers).

So in librdkafka v0.9.4 I added support for routing logs to a queue which can then be polled through a poll() call and this is what the Python client should be using to make sure the callbacks are called from a more Python-friendly context thread-wise.

There are two options here:
 * serve this log queue from a background thread, calling the callbacks on this thread.  This will provide the snappiest log experience (minimal delay between logging and displaying the logs) but it also means there will be user callbacks called on a non-main thread which might trip people up.
 * serve the log callbacks from poll(). This is the safe bet, but since it relies on the application actually calling poll() every now and then (which might not be the case outside its main loop, such as when setting up or shutting down) it means the logs will be somewhat dated when they are actually printed.
 * a third option would be to provide a builtin C callback that uses a better date format, but this won't solve the file thing.
 * post logs on some file descriptor the application can read off of (this would imply the first option as well). Seems like a lot of work for printing som logs.

I'm open to all suggestions.

someone mentioned  that

 "I ran into this problem as well. It can happen if you have PIL installed, then install Pillow on top of it.

Go to /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ and delete anything with "PIL" in the name (including directories). If the Pillow .egg file is there you might as well delete that too. Then re-install Pillow.

Those are good arguments for maintaining support for 2.6.  I'm willing to look into how hard it would be to make a 2.6-friendly release for networkx-1.10.  I don't have a Python2.6 setup right now to check for issues and fixes; it may not be that difficult.   If someone could help here we might be able to figure that out sooner than later. 

Generally I have been advocating for developing new modules with contemporary Python version style and idioms.  It makes it simpler and easier for developers.  Even though it may not be that much harder to support older versions, practically the project has to choose where to put our limited time and resources.   Fortunately, NetworkX has been growing very fast in the past few months with many new developers so the ability to develop and maintain high-quality well-tested code is expanding too.   Even so I don't expect that we are likely to want to support 2.6 significantly farther into the future.

We already subclass `logging.Formatter`, to which we could pass a custom `format` parameter, and simply use `super().format(record)` and `click.style` inside of our `format(self, record)` implementation - with getting support for all [`LogRecord` parameters](http://devdocs.io/python~3.6/library/logging#logging.LogRecord) for free as a bonus :-)

However, honoring a passed `format` string would also mean and not treating messages differently depending on a log level, [which is currently intended behaviour](https://github.com/click-contrib/click-log/pull/14#issuecomment-367536374).

In addition, we'd only be able to color the message as a whole (by calling `click.style` on the string returned from `super().format()`). Working around that by passing a copy of the original record with color escape strings on the loglevel attribute to `super().format()` looks really hacky for me.

So what's your opinion on it? I'd really like to see support for the `format` parameter and friends in `click-log`, rather than having users subclassing on their own…

The simplest solution would be option one. It's very easy to roll out a new package for a new version. You are also keeping the versions separate, so there is no chance of using parts of an old version, and bits of the new one, which could cause issues.

Option two could be done by adapting the endpoint configuration to allow you to segment a set of URIs for different versions. This would support backwards compat for people who don't want to fully upgrade yet.

I'm erring on the side of option 1 here. We keep versions explicit between other python packages anyway, so this is in keeping with that convention :)

So... this the first design that pops in my mind:

## Usage
Since is very hard to automatically predict what is the actual type of the data being deserialized (neither would this be good since wouldn't be explicit to the user), we can let the user decide how to deserialize it. eg.:
``` python
response.start_date().to_date()
response.total_cost().to_decimal()
response.other_data_type().to_other_data_type()
```
This also complies with the current design we have since we need to call 
``` python
response.start_data().data()
``` 
to access the raw data (in this case probably a date formatted string). So it feels natural for me to be able to retrieve this data in another format using the ``to_date()``.

The conversion 'trigger' would be "any method starting with 'to_' " (or we could use 'as_' . eg: ``response.start_data().as_date()``). This would make TapiocaExecutor to start look for a conversion in the TapiocaAdapter.

## Customizing conversions
We would need a new class called ``Serializer`` that handles data conversion. This should be fully customizable by developers creating wrappers. The basic implementation would look something like this:
``` python
class Serializer(object):

	def deserialize(self, method, value):
		if hasattr(self, method):
			return getattr(self, method)(value)
	
	def to_date(self, value):
		return Date(value)

	def to_decimal(self, value):
		return Decimal(value)
``` 
Note that developers are encouraged to create any `to_` methods they wish.
Serializers could then be attached to Adapters declaratively:
``` python 
class ExampleClientAdapter(...):
	serializer = Serializer()

	def get_serializer(self):
		return self.serializer
```

## Important notes
- There should be a way for the developer exploring the wrapper to discover what are the available `to_` methods.

> I'm seeing a new build warning on Python 3.6 (only)

Interesting. I think that's highly platform dependent, although I don't really understand why it started showing up. [`new_allocated` is defined as `Py_ssize_t`](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zodbpickle/blob/master/src/zodbpickle/_pickle_33.c#L214). The definition of `PY_SSIZE_T_MAX` is the same in 2.7:
```
cpython [2.7|…1]$ ack 'define PY_SSIZE_T_MAX'
Include/pyport.h
202:#define PY_SSIZE_T_MAX ((Py_ssize_t)(((size_t)-1)>>1))
```
as it is in 3.6:
```
cpython [3.6|…1]$ ack 'define PY_SSIZE_T_MAX'
Include/pyport.h
109:#define PY_SSIZE_T_MAX ((Py_ssize_t)(((size_t)-1)>>1))
```

AFAICS, `Py_ssize_t` itself is the same in [2.7](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Include/pyport.h#L178) as it is in [3.6](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Include/pyport.h#L79), at least on any platforms that have `ssize_t` defined. If that *isn't* defined, then it changes: on both versions, the fallback is to make it the same as `Py_intptr_t`, but the value of that has changed. In 3.6, it's unconditionally `intptr_t`, but 2.7 supports platforms that don't have that defined, so it could wind up as just `int`. In any case, those should all be signed.

Now, `sizeof` is always defined to return an unsigned integer (technically an unsigned integer constant). I forget the rules for C type conversions between unsigned and signed integers but I think promotion is generally to unsigned?

Does this warning go away if you cast the right hand expression to `Py_ssize_t`?

I ran into a similar issue with hierarchical softmax using the default learning rate. On the dataset I was using, if the base learning rate is too high, the gradients/embedding weight updates can explode, meaning embedding matrix elements can overflow the 32 bit float. 

I suspect this is causing the binary logistic score to equal inf (or NaN), so when [x is passed to the sigmoid function](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/e39dfbe598542fbb8a8e25be596ecab3ad25edd9/src/model.cc#L336), the array index is invalid, causing the seg fault.

May be worthwhile checking for inf/NaN and warning the user of an overflow - I think this is the approach taken in the various sklearn models.

I think a pattern of using subviews for items in a collection view is
preferable to me, both due to @indirectylit's comment about rerendering,
but also due to encapsulation of logic.

If each model has its own view, then DOM events being handled by that view
can just take for granted that they are being fired to change their model.

In the case where we use a handlebars each, we have to add extra logic (and
code model ids into the dom) in order to work out which model's part of the
template is being clicked on.

> Marionette 2.4.5 only seems to re-render automatically under some
> circumstances when you use the CollectionView. For the CollectionView I
> would indeed expect it to only re-render the items changed (though I might
> also expect it to re-render the item's entire template and not just any
> particular DOM nodes that happened to change). In all other cases it's up
> to the user to trigger a re-render, and I've done so here pretty
> aggressively. I re-render the whole app when *any* data changes. This
> makes the re-rendering logic nearly trivial, which I think is an advantage.
> Obviously this might not be such a hot idea unless we're using a virtual
> DOM, if the app becomes very large.
>
> The alternative to aggressive re-rendering + vDOM is to let each view
> carefully manage when its subviews are re-rendered. The drawback here is

This is more an issue with marshmallow-jsonapi than apispec. marshmallow-jsonapi relies on `post_dump`/`pre_load` methods for jsonapi formatting, which makes it difficult for apispec to inspect the correct schemas. This issue is documented in marshmallow-code/marshmallow-jsonapi#12. Further discussion should happen on that issue.

This also introduces a backwards-incompatible change in how method signatures are handled. Currently,  the following test passes:

```python
    class F3(object):
        def __call__(self, foo, bar):
            pass
    f3 = F3()

    for func in [f1, f2, f3]:
        assert utils.get_func_args(func) == ['self', 'foo', 'bar']
```

Using `inspect.signature` will make this test fail.

Not sure what the best solution is. This will require more thought.

This doesn't appear to be `resource`-specific.  `venusian.scan()` uses the stdlib [`inspect.getmembers()`](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/inspect.html#inspect.getmembers) function to iterate over members of modules and packages to invoke registered callables.  [`getmembers()`](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/inspect.html#inspect.getmembers) returns a **sorted** list of members, so it appears that route registration will always be alphabetic unless manually registered.


```python
from cornice.service import Service
from pyramid.config import Configurator
from pyramid.response import Response


svc_B = Service(
    name='B',
    path='api/service/always_B')

svc_A = Service(
    name='A',
    path='api/service/{parameter}')

svc_C = Service(
    name='C',
    path='api/service/{parameter}/extra')


@svc_C.get()
def c(request):
    return Response('Service C')


@svc_B.get()
def b(request):
    return Response('Service B')


@svc_A.get()
def a(request):
    return Response('Service A')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    config = Configurator()
    config.include('cornice')
    config.scan()
    
    # Manually add the services for correct order
    # config.add_cornice_service(svc_B)
    # config.add_cornice_service(svc_A)
    # config.add_cornice_service(svc_C)


    routes = config.get_routes_mapper().get_routes()
    for route in routes:
        print('URL: "{}" -> service: "{}".'.format(route.pattern, route.name))
    
    route_names = [route.name for route in routes]
    assert route_names == ['A', 'B', 'C'] 
```

I see. Why not indeed! Unless I miss a point, the downside is that swagger cannot be an «external» option anymore. The `validators` are coupled to the fact that we want swagger enabled on the API. It could become problematic when dealing with several formats outputs (e.g. API-blueprint)..

My original idea was to leave the original `schema` kwarg as a descriptive attribute storing an agnostic schema for the service — without exploiting it as we do now. But I realize it can be pretty confusing if it's name is just `schema` as before.

I propose that we come back to this topic in another issue/pull-request, even if it means that we delay the swagger feature.

Getting rid of the current `schema` approach and pushing towards a flat and simple list of validators makes a lot of sense right now IMO.

I have found the bug and it was in the scripts I used to launch bwa. When I generated the RG string Y used this: 
rg_str = "@RG\tID:{ID}\tSM:{SM}\tPL:{PL}\tLB:{LB}".format(**readgroup)
When bwa is executed python "translates" \t to {tab} and bwa writes the tab into the header. In a post processing of the bam the order of the tab separated items in the PG line is changed. And this is producing my error.

To solve that I have changed the rg_str to:
rg_str = r"@RG\tID:{ID}\tSM:{SM}\tPL:{PL}\tLB:{LB}".format(**readgroup)

It produces a PG line without {tab}s inside the CL "item".

I am going to close the issue.

While your patch probably covers the state, the value is still an issue.

In this case, the missing bit of information is how the variable was set in the first place. If the gcloud ... set command is run using the `--is-text` flag, the resource returned will have a `text` field instead of a `value` field. The current implementation ignores the possibility of the `text` field. The `value` getter should check for it and return it unmodified if it's present. Alternatively, could add a `Variable.text` getter and make the user figure it out.

The secure_redirects sysctl is dependent on accept_redirects being set to 1.  If accept_redirects is set to 0, then the value of secure_redirects has no effect.

The reason Google sets secure_redirects to 1 (also the kernel default) is as an additional security measure in the event that accept_redirects is ever set to 1 (intentionally or not).  If accept_redirects is set to 1 and secure_redirects is set to 0, as described in the CIS7 benchmark, then users would be immediately susceptible to ICMP redirects from any host.  

In your case for compliance, as long as accept_redirects is set to 0, then you can set secure_redirects to any value you want, but Google recommends using the default of 1 as an additional protection.

@barry-scott I believe you are right - the code has been growing just to accommodate all the needs as they arose. This will eventually erode clarity unless some major refactoring or even a rewrite is done.

A good starting point would be to clearly separate the orthogonal parts. Something that comes to mind immediately is the part that streams objects through the git process (a hash goes in, an object comes out, the process is held alive permanently). Another obvious step could be to remove all reliance on the destructors and use context-managers or something like an idempotent `release()` method instead.

On the bright side, one can stake small steps in this endeavour, knowing that the massive reliance on the implementation will show any issues right away when running the tests, some of which might have to be adjusted at some point.

In any case, I find it most important to at most deprecate old parts of the API, so that existing clients have time to adjust. Ideally, they won't even have to, as many will just use it as a simple call wrapper.

When reading through the list of bullet points, one seems to be new in particular: `allow prompts for input to be processed (for credentials)`. I wonder whether this actually has (or should ever) work through git itself. AFAIK, there is the [`GIT_ASKPASS`](https://www.kernel.org/pub/software/scm/git/docs/gitcredentials.html) environment variable just for that purpose.

This project had a mailing list before, and I closed it just because there was nothing happening. By now I do prefer using issues on github for all public communication. If you wish to go ahead with certain refactorings or improvements, you could open an issue for them and discuss the details there. Even though the process is not well defined, we will figure something out as we go, there isn't too many people active on this project after all. I hope that will work for you, as your contributions are greatly appreciated.

### Naming

`ratio` does not apply anymore. It was true at the 0.0.1 of `imblearn` but we support multi classes since then. Do you think it is worth to rename it to something more related to what it does. Any input is welcomed. ping @jorisvandenbossche since it does not like the naming ;)


### API

Right now, we can pass some `string`, `dict` and callable. We decided to do so to not have 2 arguments (targeted classes and #samples to get) avoid tedious sanity check between those arguments (length, order, etc.). Right now, we have an issue with the cleaning under-sampling method which discard the number of samples to select and just considered the class. This is bringing some confusion to users.

So if you see a way to make this parameter easier to manipulate and intuitive for the end-user, it would be great.
